text: The surgeon observes loops of small bowel tethered within the pelvic region, indicating adhesions or anatomical constraints.
Loops of small bowel are visibly tethered in the pelvis, indicating their restricted movement within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses their left hand to retract the colon while carefully separating loops of small bowel from adhesions in the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses their left hand to retract the colon while carefully detaching loops of small bowel from adhesions in the pelvic region.
The surgeon repairs serosal tears on the loops of small bowel, with visible stitches present on the bowel surface.
The surgeon repairs serosal tears on the small bowel loops, which were previously observed to be tethered in the pelvis.
The video shows the fused sigmoid mesentery attached to the retroperitoneum over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon identifies the fused sigmoid mesentery attached to the retroperitoneum over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon surveys the surgical field, marking the intended area for resection.
The surgeon surveys the surgical field, marking the intended area for resection in preparation for the colectomy.
The surgeon begins the procedure by approaching laterally, preparing to dissect and mobilize the relevant anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins the procedure by approaching the surgical site laterally, preparing for the resection in the colectomy.
The surgeon presses on the superior aspect of the stent and continues the dissection down towards the dome of the structure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, progressing down to the dome of the bladder, carefully separating tissue layers in this area.
The surgeon encounters dense adhesions between the colon, stent, and bladder, resembling the typical presentation in cases of perforated diverticulitis with a colovesical fistula.
The surgeon identifies the stent and attempts to move laterally, navigating around the anatomical structures.
The surgeon visually identifies the stent within the surgical field, noting its position relative to surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon attempts to dissect laterally, navigating dense adhesions between the colon, stent, and bladder.
The surgeon uses ureteral catheters and administers ICG dye, which is visualized to confirm the ureter's location and ensure the bladder remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon has placed ureteral catheters and injected indocyanine green (ICG) dye, which may be visible in the surgical field to enhance visualization of anatomical structures.
The surgeon attempts to separate the adhered colon from the bladder, revealing wires from the stent visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon directly addresses the dense adherence between the colon and the bladder, carefully dissecting the tissue to separate the two structures.
The surgeon points out the wires of the stent, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates below the inflammatory reaction surrounding the area, preparing to create a new wide open anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully dissects below the area of inflammatory reaction, working to separate the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares to create a new wide open anastomosis, ensuring clear visibility of the surgical site.

The surgeon navigates down the right side, revealing the stent that has eroded through the tissue. They carefully attempt to establish a dissection plane above the colon wall, avoiding perforation of the bladder.
The surgeon navigates down the right side of the surgical field, likely maneuvering around inflamed tissue to continue the dissection.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, attempting to establish a plane above the colon wall where a stent has visibly eroded through, while carefully avoiding perforation of the adjacent bladder.
The surgeon begins to identify a proper dissection plane between the colon and bladder, carefully navigating around the visible stent wires embedded in the tissue.
The surgeon begins to identify a proper dissection plane near the colon, carefully navigating to avoid damage to adjacent structures.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to navigate dense fibrotic adherence, attempting to find a surgical plane as they work down into the pelvis and proceed to the opposite side.
The surgeon attempts to locate the surgical plane while dissecting down into the pelvis, carefully navigating around dense fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon works on the left side of the surgical field, continuing to find and dissect the appropriate plane around the colon while avoiding damage to adjacent structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the colon anteriorly, separating it from the bladder, following the principles of working from known to unknown areas in challenging surgeries.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully dissect and mobilize the colon anteriorly, separating it from the bladder.
The surgeon navigates between the bladder and the anterior wall of the colon, aiming to reach the normal rectum beneath while ensuring the dissection is below the level of the stent.
The surgeon briefly enters the wall of the bladder before returning focus to the anterior wall of the colon, using robotic instruments to navigate the dense fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects deeper into the pelvic region, aiming to expose the normal rectum below the level of the stent.
The surgeon identifies the mesorectum, a wad of fat, and locates the proper plane for resection, planning to move laterally to avoid complications.
The surgeon identifies the need to move laterally to the wad of fat, which is revealed to be the mesorectum.
The surgeon identifies the proper anatomical plane between the colon and surrounding structures, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon takes the mesentery to the mid rectum, ensuring it is below the stent seated in the mid rectum. The splenic flexure has been remobilized to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon has reached beneath the mesorectum and proceeds to dissect the mesentery, separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon has completed lateral and medial dissection of the colon, elevating the structure for further surgical access.
The surgeon remobilizes the splenic flexure to ensure adequate access for the procedure, adjusting the position of the colon to reach lower areas.
The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery down to the mid rectum, ensuring the area is prepared for an attention-free anastomosis below the stent seated in the mid rectum.
The surgeon clears the mesentery, bringing it up to the bowel wall to create a smooth surface for the stapler, ensuring minimal bleeding risk.
The surgeon palpates the area and clears the mesentery, focusing on the region around the mid rectum.
The surgeon carefully dissects up to the bowel wall, preparing a smooth surface for the stapler while ensuring minimal bleeding.
The surgeon opens the anterior wall of the rectum to visually confirm the distal extent of the stent, ensuring it is not inadvertently stapled during the procedure.
The surgeon opens the anterior wall of the rectum to visually confirm the absence of the stent and assess the distal extent before proceeding with stapling.
The surgeon visually identifies the distal aspect of the stent within the rectum.
The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the rectum, ensuring complete division across the tissue. The stapling process requires an additional application to fully cross the rectal wall.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the prepared surface of the bowel wall and begins the stapling process.
The surgeon applies an additional staple to ensure complete closure across the rectal tissue, confirming the absence of the stent before proceeding.
The surgeon cleans up the proximal margin of the bowel, located just proximal to the stent, without needing to open this section. The bowel is then divided.
The surgeon cleans up the proximal margin of the bowel, located just proximal to the stent, without needing to open this section of the bowel.
The surgeon divides the tissue at the distal aspect of the stent, completing the separation of the bowel segment.
The surgeon attempts to remobilize the splenic flexure, facing challenges due to anatomical constraints.
The surgeon achieves an attention-free anastomosis, indicating the successful connection of the bowel segments without tension.

The surgeon joins the two staple lines using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, initially showing a gap between them. With two throws, the suture is used to gently approximate the ends, which meet easily. The patient remains in a steep Trendelenburg position during this anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to join the two staple lines, which are visibly spaced apart.
The surgeon performs two throws with the suture to gently approximate the two ends of the bowel, which come together easily.
The surgeon observes the small bowel filling the pelvis after removing the patient from the Trendelenburg position.
The surgeon joins the two posterior staple lines, ensuring there is no tension in the anastomosis, which will be tested near the end of the procedure.
The surgeon joins the two posterior staple lines, completing the anastomosis in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a proctotomy in preparation for a two-layer anastomosis, creating an opening in the rectal tissue.
The surgeon begins performing a two-layer anastomosis, preparing to join the bowel segments with two distinct layers of sutures.
The surgeon performs a proctotomy, making an incision in the rectal tissue as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon operates using three robotic arms, with the first arm holding the small bowel in the right lower quadrant, pressed against two sponges to keep it out of the surgical field.
The surgeon operates using three robotic arms, including a camera and two working arms, to perform the procedure.
The surgeon uses the first robotic arm to hold the small bowel in the right lower quadrant, pressing it against two sponges to keep it out of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the inner layer of the anastomosis using three robotic arms, as the fourth arm was not needed for this step.
The surgeon performs the inner layer of a two-layer anastomosis during the colectomy procedure, focusing on precise suturing within the surgical field.
The surgeon adjoins the lip created between the otomy and the posterior staple lines during the anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon adjoins the lip between the otomy and the staple lines at the posterior aspect, ensuring alignment of the tissue edges.
The surgeon performs the posterior inner row of the anastomosis, with clear exposure allowing precise placement of stitches along the posterior staple lines.
The surgeon constructs the posterior inner row of the anastomosis, aligning and securing the tissue layers.
The surgeon places stitches precisely along the intended line, ensuring accurate alignment within the surgical field.

The surgeon rocks back and forth between the left and right hands to adjust the angle, preparing to start the canal over the top. The second inner suture is initiated, with tension being checked on the suture line.
The surgeon begins creating a canal over the top of the exposed tissue, preparing for further dissection or manipulation.
The surgeon begins placing the second inner suture, with visible precision in the stitching technique.

The surgeon uses the left hand to sew around the near corner, taking large bites to form the inner anterior row. The technique involves cannelling over the top with small bites of seromuscular tissue, occasionally catching some mucosa, and extending the suture across to meet the other suture.
The surgeon sews around the near corner of the surgical site, taking large bites of tissue with the suture needle.
The surgeon switches to using the left hand to continue the suturing process, adjusting the angle for optimal technique.
The surgeon continues cannelling over the top, guiding the suture through the tissue layers to secure the surgical site.
The surgeon forms the inner anterior row by taking small bites of seromuscular tissue and occasionally catching some mucosa, creating a canal-like structure.
The surgeon brings the suture across to meet the other suture, completing the stitching of the tissue.
The surgeon ties the stitches together after catching the mucosa, then uses the original suture to create an imbricating lumbar suture over the staple lines, completing the two-layer anastomosis.
The surgeon ties together the previously placed stitches, completing the inner anterior row of sutures that include small bites of seromuscular tissue and some mucosa.
The surgeon takes the original suture from the staple lines and sutures it as an imbricating lumbar suture over the top, reinforcing the anastomosis site.
The surgeon completes a full two-layer anastomosis, ensuring the surgical connection between bowel segments is secure.
